title: Geometric Quantum Physics Informed Neural Network
authors: Wai-Hong Tam, Reza Safari, Hiromichi Matsuyama
abstract: Quantum physics-informed neural networks (QPINNs) have recently emerged as a promising framework for the solution of partial differential equations (PDEs), with several studies reporting improved convergence and accuracy relative to classical physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) at reduced training cost. Motivated by these advances, we introduce geometric quantum physics-informed neural networks (GQPINNs), a symmetry-aware extension of QPINNs in which the geometric structure of the underlying PDE is incorporated directly into the quantum-circuit ansatz. Building on the framework of geometric quantum machine learning, we construct parametrized circuits that encode finite-group and compact Lie-group symmetries as inductive biases through problem-specific equivariant generator sets . Using a twirling-based construction, we derive symmetry-preserving gates that ensure that the model predictions respect the symmetries of the governing equation whenever the boundary and initial data are symmetry compatible. We benchmark GQPINNs against standard QPINNs and symmetry-adapted classical PINN baselines under matched training protocols across a representative set of linear and nonlinear PDEs. Across these benchmarks, GQPINNs achieve improved solution accuracy, as quantified by lower mean absolute error, while requiring substantially fewer trainable parameters. These results identify symmetry-aware quantum-circuit design as an effective route toward improved efficiency and generalization in quantum PDE solvers and provide a systematic framework for incorporating geometric inductive biases into quantum-enhanced scientific machine learning.
summary: This paper introduces Geometric Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks (GQPINNs), which incorporate spatial symmetries directly into quantum circuit architectures. By using symmetry as an inductive bias, the method achieves much higher accuracy and requires fewer parameters than standard quantum neural networks for solving PDEs. This represents a significant step toward more efficient and physically consistent quantum-enhanced scientific machine learning.
main_problem: Improving the efficiency, accuracy, and generalization of Quantum Physics-Informed Neural Networks (QPINNs) for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) by addressing high parameter counts and optimization difficulties.
main_result: The proposed GQPINNs achieve significantly higher accuracy (up to two orders of magnitude lower MAE) and better parameter efficiency than standard QPINNs by embedding geometric symmetries directly into the quantum circuit architecture.
method: The authors use geometric quantum machine learning principles and a twirling-based construction to embed finite-group (K4) and compact Lie-group (SO(2)) symmetries into the quantum circuit ansatz as inductive biases.
model_or_system: A variational quantum machine learning framework using a data re-uploading architecture with parametrized quantum circuits (PQCs) designed to be G-invariant or G-equivariant.
key_observables: The expectation value of a chosen operator O (e.g., Pauli strings) representing the PDE solution u(x, t).
important_parameters: Number of qubits, number of layers, number of trainable parameters, and diffusion coefficient D.
main_assumptions: The model assumes that the boundary and initial data are symmetry-compatible with the embedded geometric structure; focus is on the NISQ regime.
figures_summary: Figures show MAE as a function of trainable parameters, comparisons of accuracy between different symmetry-embedded models (K4, SO(2), Z2) and classical baselines, and the temporal evolution of the diffusion equation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the GQPINN framework, details the symmetry-embedding construction via twirling, applies the method to various PDEs (Poisson, Diffusion, Acoustic Wave, Burgers), benchmarks performance against QPINN and classical PINN, and discusses scaling and future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Baseline QPINN ansatz based on the data re-uploading scheme [35]. The circuit alternates trainable blocks and encoding blocks applied to inputs (x1, . . . , xn−1, t), repeated for p layers, followed by a final trainable block Wp+1 and measurement to produce the model output uθ(x, t).

FIG. 2. K4-GQPINN ansatz enforcing invariance under coordinate exchange (x, y) 7→(y, x) and simultaneous parity inversion (x, y) 7→(−x, −y). The trainable block is constructed to be K4-equivariant using the symmetry-preserving generator set TK4. The coordinates (x, y) are encoded onto the two qubits via angle encoding using RY rotations.

FIG. 3. SO(2)-GQPINN ansatz enforcing invariance under rotations in the (x, y) plane. The overall circuit structure follows Fig. 2, with the trainable block constructed from the symmetry-preserving generator set TSO(2). The coordinates (x, y) are encoded onto the qubits using the Bloch-sphere encoding unitary ˜U(x, y) in Eq. (43).

FIG. 4. The extension of SO(2)-GQPINN ansatz for time-dependent PDEs. The spatial qubits follows the SO(2)-GQPINN structure in Fig. 3, while the time qubit follows the baseline QPINN structure in Fig. 1 with time encoded via RZ(t). The only additional components are two RZZ couplings, parameterized by θ8 and θ9,that entangle the spatial and temporal registers to enable spatiotemporal interactions.
FIG. 5. MAE of the QPINN, K4-GQPINN, and SO(2)- GQPINN ansatzes on the 2D Poisson equation across different numbers of trainable parameters.
